content: it works

I had the same problem and I solved in that way:
  1. Create a new app through 'flutter create testProject'
  2. Remove 'ios' folder in your old project
  3. Copy the ios folder from the new project to your old one (move 'testProject/ios' to 'oldProject/ios')
Then with 'flutter run' everything worked well.

Hope that this solution could help

So I tried the suggestion by @mravn-google and got the missing Flutter.h error to go away, however it introduced another issue. All of the plugins were moved in `ios/.symlinks/plugins` and xcode can't find them. 

Is there a way I can set the directory where the plugins should be looked for? The failing lines in xcode just look like this:

#import <cloud_firestore/CloudFirestorePlugin.h>

(along with other package imports).

Thanks!

There should be no need for auxiliary configuration to use plugins, beyond the `Podfile` that is distributed with Flutter. If you are having problems based on the project templates of the Flutter beta or master branches, please help us resolve it by filing a new issue, including the output of `flutter doctor -v`.

Hi, I am not sure, But I think that I've tried all solutions I can find on this issue. I am totally new to Flutter and just running the basic test projects. Everything worked as a charm until i tried to start the app on my iPhone and had to enter information in the "xcode Runner". Before that on an Android device and in the simulator it worked fine.

Now it produces different errors but mostly I end up with the "Flutter/flutter.h" not found problem. (Only thing that works now is to run on Android phone) Please help me with this error, I've put more than a day of work to sort this problem out. Deleted all projects, uninstalling cocoapods and reinstalling it, trying different versions and so on.. Nothing helps, And oh, Flutter doctor is happy as always.

For anyone who also has issues with the Flutter.h not found, check your Flutter version. I had the 1.4 Flutter version and uninstalled it and then installed the stable version (1.2) and it worked.

I'm experiencing this on one of our build machines. I've tried reinstalling Flutter, switching to the stable channel, making sure cocoapods is the same version as on the working machines and pretty much every other solution suggested. The only temporary success I've had is with yxfmlbb's fix in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/24428. The Flutter.framework header files are replaced with aliases that lead nowhere when I run build --release, and after that the iOS build fails with Flutter.h file not found.

I can only assume I haven't reinstalled Flutter completely. What else should I be doing besides deleting the Flutter installation directory?

Edit: this appears to be some weird Jenkins issue as running the same commands from terminal does not overwrite the header files with shortcuts.

Hopefully this helps someone->

I fixed this by doing pod install in my iOS project.

It seemed it was caused by updating flutter and then the podspec for flutter being out of date.

## Native assets support for MacOS and iOS

Support for FFI calls with `@Native external` functions through Native assets on MacOS and iOS. This enables bundling native code without any build-system boilerplate code.

For more info see:

* https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/129757

### Implementation details for MacOS and iOS.

Dylibs are bundled by (1) making them fat binaries if multiple architectures are targeted, (2) code signing these, and (3) copying them to the frameworks folder. These steps are done manual rather than via CocoaPods. CocoaPods would have done the same steps, but (a) needs the dylibs to be there before the `xcodebuild` invocation (we could trick it, by having a minimal dylib in the place and replace it during the build process, that works), and (b) can't deal with having no dylibs to be bundled (we'd have to bundle a dummy dylib or include some dummy C code in the build file).

The dylibs are build as a new target inside flutter assemble, as that is the moment we know what build-mode and architecture to target.

The mapping from asset id to dylib-path is passed in to every kernel compilation path. The interesting case is hot-restart where the initial kernel file is compiled by the "inner" flutter assemble, while after hot restart the "outer" flutter run compiled kernel file is pushed to the device. Both kernel files need to contain the mapping. The "inner" flutter assemble gets its mapping from the NativeAssets target which builds the native assets. The "outer" flutter run get its mapping from a dry-run invocation. Since this hot restart can be used for multiple target devices (`flutter run -d all`) it contains the mapping for all known targets.

### Example vs template

The PR includes a new template that uses the new native assets in a package and has an app importing that. Separate discussion in: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/131209.

### Tests

This PR adds new tests to cover the various use cases.

* dev/devicelab/bin/tasks/native_assets_ios.dart
  * Runs an example app with native assets in all build modes, doing hot reload and hot restart in debug mode.
* dev/devicelab/bin/tasks/native_assets_ios_simulator.dart
  * Runs an example app with native assets, doing hot reload and hot restart.
* packages/flutter_tools/test/integration.shard/native_assets_test.dart
  * Runs (incl hot reload/hot restart), builds, builds frameworks for iOS, MacOS and flutter-tester.
* packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/build_system/targets/native_assets_test.dart
  * Unit tests the new Target in the backend.
* packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/ios/native_assets_test.dart
* packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/macos/native_assets_test.dart
  * Unit tests the native assets being packaged on a iOS/MacOS build.

It also extends various existing tests:

* dev/devicelab/bin/tasks/module_test_ios.dart
   * Exercises the add2app scenario.
* packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/features_test.dart
   * Unit test the new feature flag.

## Pre-launch Checklist

- [x] I read the [Contributor Guide] and followed the process outlined there for submitting PRs.
- [x] I read the [Tree Hygiene] wiki page, which explains my responsibilities.
- [x] I read and followed the [Flutter Style Guide], including [Features we expect every widget to implement].
- [x] I signed the [CLA].
- [x] I listed at least one issue that this PR fixes in the description above.
- [x] I updated/added relevant documentation (doc comments with `///`).
- [x] I added new tests to check the change I am making, or this PR is [test-exempt].
- [x] All existing and new tests are passing.

<!-- Links -->
[Contributor Guide]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Tree-hygiene#overview
[Tree Hygiene]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Tree-hygiene
[test-exempt]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Tree-hygiene#tests
[Flutter Style Guide]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Style-guide-for-Flutter-repo
[Features we expect every widget to implement]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Style-guide-for-Flutter-repo#features-we-expect-every-widget-to-implement
[CLA]: https://cla.developers.google.com/
[flutter/tests]: https://github.com/flutter/tests
[breaking change policy]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Tree-hygiene#handling-breaking-changes
[Discord]: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/wiki/Chat

Thanks @vashworth !

Some high level replies to your questions below.

> Question: Is the template meant to be used by users or just added added for the sake of testing?

I'd like either a sample project or a template.

A template is more discoverable than a sample project. I worry that we will push users towards using the old (and when native assets are no longer experimental) FFI plugin template if we don't have a new template.

(A sample project could be in https://github.com/dart-lang/native/tree/main/pkgs/native_assets_cli/example, however having the Flutter app also there would mean we cannot unit test it on the CI of that repo.)

Maybe we can somehow get all the benefits of a template without all the machinery? Suggestions?

> Seems to be this will only work with [Option B for Add to App](https://docs.flutter.dev/add-to-app/ios/project-setup#option-b---embed-frameworks-in-xcode). Is this going to be a known limitation that you can only use native assets with Add to App if you do Option B?

I wasn't aware of the other options.

Maybe we can make the `podhelper.rb` also do copying. I had that in an earlier prototype I believe. (Though I'm not so fluent in Ruby so that was crashing when there were no native assets. And it requires us to pass some information somewhere.)

> I didn’t see any macOS devicelab tests. Any reason why not? If not, add them?

All the MacOS tests are in test/integration.shard/native_assets_test.dart

Maybe that is the wrong place for tests that run a MacOS application? Should they be in a different place?

Also, currently that test also runs `flutter build ios`, but it [fails on the CI](https://logs.chromium.org/logs/flutter/buildbucket/cr-buildbucket/8774707149105331809/+/u/run_test.dart_for_tool_integration_tests_shard_and_subshard_3_4/test_stdout):

```
02:57 +26 -1: test/integration.shard/native_assets_test.dart: flutter build ios with native assets release [E]                                                                                         
  Exception: flutter build failed: 1
  Error (Xcode): No signing certificate "iOS Development" found: No "iOS Development" signing certificate matching team ID "S8QB4VV633" with a private key was found.
```

Maybe this is also a result of running a test on the wrong shard/bot? Should building ios apps also happen in the iOS device lab instead of on an ordinary MacOS machine?

@dcharkes I looked over the App to App solutions again, and I think it should work with your design actually. [Option A](https://docs.flutter.dev/add-to-app/ios/project-setup#option-a---embed-with-cocoapods-and-the-flutter-sdk) adds script phases which call xcode_backend.sh, like a normal flutter project, so your code should be fine.

https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/master/packages/flutter_tools/templates/module/ios/library/Flutter.tmpl/podhelper.rb.tmpl#L92-L100

And looking closer at [Option C](https://docs.flutter.dev/add-to-app/ios/project-setup#option-c---embed-application-and-plugin-frameworks-in-xcode-and-flutter-framework-with-cocoapods), plugins still need to be embedded manually so the same would be true for native assets, so that also should be fine.

Therefore, I believe all three options should be compatible. 

Also, here's an integration tests used in the tool for add to app (Looks like it uses Option A): https://github.com/flutter/flutter/tree/master/dev/integration_tests/ios_add2app_life_cycle 
https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/5554b0eeb3780d3fac90370d75dc57893733ae71/dev/bots/test.dart#L762-L773

Looks like Option B is only tested by ensuring the frameworks are created and exist (https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/master/dev/devicelab/bin/tasks/build_ios_framework_module_test.dart)

> Therefore, I believe all three options should be compatible.

Great!

> Also, here's an integration tests used in the tool for add to app (Looks like it uses Option A): https://github.com/flutter/flutter/tree/master/dev/integration_tests/ios_add2app_life_cycle

I'm not sure that this test is actually run, as it is broken for me locally. I've filed https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/131261 to follow up.

> Looks like Option B is only tested by ensuring the frameworks are created and exist (https://github.com/flutter/flutter/blob/master/dev/devicelab/bin/tasks/build_ios_framework_module_test.dart)

The PR has a test similar to that in test/integration.shard/native_assets_test.dart, see `expectDylibIsBundledWithFrameworks`.

> Add tests to ensure dry run happens in the following tests:
> packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/ios/mac_test.dart
> packages/flutter_tools/test/commands.shard/hermetic/build_macos_test.dart
> packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/hot_test.dart

What does dry-run mean in this context @vashworth? Tests which run with the memory file system and mock process run?

I believe I would need to either (1) address https://github.com/dart-lang/native/issues/90, or (2) make the code in packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/macos/native_assets.dart and packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/native_assets.dart sit inside a class, and create mocks for that class and add that class as a field to `class BuildCommand` and a bunch of other places.

I believe approach (1) might be better, because we'd actually be testing that `flutter_tools` invokes the `dart path/to/some/build.dart` of the various packages that have native assets that need to be build. With approach (2) we'd just be returning some boolean or a path to a native_assets.yaml which would not be catching many errors.

> I believe approach (1) might be better, because we'd actually be testing that flutter_tools invokes the dart path/to/some/build.dart of the various packages that have native assets that need to be build. With approach (2) we'd just be returning some boolean or a path to a native_assets.yaml which would not be catching many errors.

With approach (1) we'd have to mock a variety of process calls and create a bunch of files on these process calls (https://github.com/dart-lang/native/issues/90#issuecomment-1651352057). That would make these tests rather involved,  mimicking pub and other tools.

I'm not entirely sure where to put the mock boundary with approach (2). We could wrap both `NativeAssetsBuilder` and `PackageConfig` and make mocks for those. But that would also mean these would have to be passed around everywhere or be dependency injected through `Environment`. Passing around seems like a bad idea. But I also don't see any other examples of dependencies of flutter_tools being dependency injected. Do we have an example of mocking a dependency somewhere in flutter_tools? @stuartmorgan @vashworth.

Without mocking, the unit testing as requested is rather limited:

> Add tests to ensure dry run happens in the following tests:
> * packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/ios/mac_test.dart
> * packages/flutter_tools/test/commands.shard/hermetic/build_macos_test.dart
> * packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/hot_test.dart

These tests already cover the paths where there are no native assets, and all the extra code (a path to a yaml, a xcode property etc.) is null in that case.

> Also, add unit tests for
>
> * packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/build_system/targets/native_assets.dart 
> * packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/native_assets.dart
> * packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/macos/native_assets.dart

I've added unit tests for these, but they can only unit test the behavior that does not actually invoke the `package:native_assets_builder` dependency, so the unit tests are rather limited.  (The integration tests for MacOS and iOS should cover all this behavior.)

For the add2app test alternative I've run into another existing issue. https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/112033

The three remaining things before this PR can be merged:

1. Unit tests - Blocked by how to mock `package:native_assets_builder`. I need some guidance/example of how to do this in flutter_tools.
2. Add2app integration test actually exercising the bundled libraries - blocked by https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/112033 and https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/80807. I'm not sure I know how to make progress here.
3. Code formatting - I'd rather do this as the final step.

> I'm not entirely sure where to put the mock boundary with approach (2). We could wrap both `NativeAssetsBuilder` and `PackageConfig` and make mocks for those. But that would also mean these would have to be passed around everywhere or be dependency injected through `Environment`. Passing around seems like a bad idea. But I also don't see any other examples of dependencies of flutter_tools being dependency injected. Do we have an example of mocking a dependency somewhere in flutter_tools?

I'm not sure of any examples of mocking a dependency. @christopherfujino Do you know of any?

> > Add tests to ensure dry run happens in the following tests:
> > packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/ios/mac_test.dart
> > packages/flutter_tools/test/commands.shard/hermetic/build_macos_test.dart
> > packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/hot_test.dart
> 
> What does dry-run mean in this context @vashworth? Tests which run with the memory file system and mock process run?
> 
> I believe I would need to either (1) address [dart-lang/native#90](https://github.com/dart-lang/native/issues/90), or (2) make the code in packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/macos/native_assets.dart and packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/native_assets.dart sit inside a class, and create mocks for that class and add that class as a field to `class BuildCommand` and a bunch of other places.

Could we create a fake `NativeAssetsBuildRunner` that we pass to the command?

Also, I think you should create a wrapper class anyway that you pass in the logger, processManager, and fileSystem, so that for tests we can pass test instances. We're migrating the codebase off of using any more `globals.*` getters, and introducing new invocations is not allowed.

> > > Add tests to ensure dry run happens in the following tests:
> > > packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/ios/mac_test.dart
> > > packages/flutter_tools/test/commands.shard/hermetic/build_macos_test.dart
> > > packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/hot_test.dart
> > 
> > 
> > What does dry-run mean in this context @vashworth? Tests which run with the memory file system and mock process run?
> > I believe I would need to either (1) address [dart-lang/native#90](https://github.com/dart-lang/native/issues/90), or (2) make the code in packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/macos/native_assets.dart and packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/native_assets.dart sit inside a class, and create mocks for that class and add that class as a field to `class BuildCommand` and a bunch of other places.
> 
> Could we create a fake `NativeAssetsBuildRunner` that we pass to the command?
> 
> Also, I think you should create a wrapper class anyway that you pass in the logger, processManager, and fileSystem, so that for tests we can pass test instances. We're migrating the codebase off of using any more `globals.*` getters, and introducing new invocations is not allowed.

Okay, I'll give this a spin tomorrow. It does mean we have to add a `NativeAssetsBuildRunner` to every class/function (e.g. all the `Command`s for example) from `main` and where we pass it in tests and we want to exercise the mock. Without some kind of dependency injection (a la globals) that might be a lot of threading.

Thinking of it, the `FakeResidentCompiler` is a mock of an external dependency. This one is passed as a field of `FlutterDevice`.

> Could we create a fake `NativeAssetsBuildRunner` that we pass to the command?
> 
> Also, I think you should create a wrapper class anyway that you pass in the logger, processManager, and fileSystem, so that for tests we can pass test instances. We're migrating the codebase off of using any more `globals.*` getters, and introducing new invocations is not allowed.

Done. This is a bit verbose though adding fields and arguments in methods/constructor calls. @christopherfujino is this the preferred way of doing this?

This does not enable testing the `NativeAssets` `Target`. The targets all have const constructors and all use the dependency injection through globals. The documentation on `Target` reads:

```dart
/// ## Unit testing
///
/// Most targets will invoke an external binary which makes unit testing
/// trickier. It is recommend that for unit testing that a Fake is used and
/// provided via the dependency injection system. a [Testbed] may be used to
/// set up the environment before the test is run. Unit tests should fully
/// exercise the rule, ensuring that the existing input and output verification
/// logic can run, as well as verifying it correctly handles provided defines
/// and meets any additional contracts present in the target.
```

If I'm not mistaken the only way to use those overrides from a `context` are through `globals` such as `ProcessManager get processManager => context.get<ProcessManager>() ?? _kLocalProcessManager;`. Any suggestions @christopherfujino on how to add unit tests for a target that needs to mock its dependencies?

> packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/ios/mac_test.dart

I believe the tests should be in test/commands.shard/hermetic/build_ios_test.dart instead, no invocations of `buildXcodeProject` are tested in packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/ios/mac_test.dart. I've added a test in test/commands.shard/hermetic/build_ios_test.dart.

> packages/flutter_tools/test/commands.shard/hermetic/build_macos_test.dart

Done.

> packages/flutter_tools/test/general.shard/hot_test.dart

I could only figure out how to do a hot restart in here. `run` isn't implemented in the fake

I also tried add a test to test/general.shard/resident_runner_test.dart (another place where `HotRunner` is constructed and run). I could only figure out how to `run`. For `restart` and `reload` I can't figure out what the `fakeVmServiceHost` expects. Maybe this test has the wrong fakes for just testing native assets. @vashworth what exactly would you like to seen unit tested for the hot runner? Is there already a similar test?

> Also, add unit tests for
> 
> packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/build_system/targets/native_assets.dart

See discussion above about dependency injection.

> packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/ios/native_assets.dart
> packages/flutter_tools/lib/src/macos/native_assets.dart

Done.

> @dcharkes If you want to try and add this, you can go for it. Alternatively, it looks like `ios_host_app` is also an Add to App integration test (used in `module_test_ios.dart`). Perhaps use that?

_Originally posted in https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/131261#issuecomment-1650058595_

It seems that we lose the `NATIVE_ASSETS` env var setting the path to `native_assets.yaml` for the kernel compilation in `Build iOS Objective-C host app`. This is seen by the `flutter assemble` call having `-dNativeAssets= ` and no `NATIVE_ASSETS = ...` in the preceding lines. It looks like there is no xcconfig because the  `PACKAGE_CONFIG = ` in the log lines of these non-flutter originated invocations is missing (which is ones of the other defines in the xcconfig file). Is it a constraint for add2app that it should work without our generated xcconfig? @vashworth

> @dcharkes I looked over the App to App solutions again, and I think it should work with your design actually. [Option A](https://docs.flutter.dev/add-to-app/ios/project-setup#option-a---embed-with-cocoapods-and-the-flutter-sdk) adds script phases which call xcode_backend.sh, like a normal flutter project, so your code should be fine.

It doesn't seem entirely true. I believe there is no xcconfig which would set the right environment variables.

If we can't pass information through `xcconfig`, we have some options:

1. Do a dry-run invocation inside `KernelSnapshot` to produce a native_assets.yaml there. The downdside of this is that a `flutter run` invocation will now do a dry run both in the "outer" run and "inner" assemble invocation. Also it does a non-dry-run in the "inner" invocation.
   1.b. In addition keep passing native_assets.yaml in the xcconfig, and skip running in the `KernelSnapshot` if we already have one. (This requires us to pass an empty native_assets.yaml in projects without any native assets, instead of no native_assets.yaml that we do now.)
2. Make the `KernelSnapshot` target depend on the `NativeAssets` target and use the non-dry-run native_assets.yaml. The downside of this is that now `KernelSnapshot` caching doesn't work anymore. `NativeAssets` targets don't have caching.

Edit: A second issue seems to be that `flutter clean` removes the `build` folder which contains `\build/native_assets/ios/native_assets.yaml`. So it seems we should not rely on anything an "outer" flutter invocation leaves on the file system for an "inner" assemble invocation.

> I also tried add a test to test/general.shard/resident_runner_test.dart (another place where HotRunner is constructed and run). I could only figure out how to run. For restart and reload I can't figure out what the fakeVmServiceHost expects. Maybe this test has the wrong fakes for just testing native assets. @vashworth what exactly would you like to seen unit tested for the hot runner? Is there already a similar test?

Yeah, looks like resident_runner_test.dart is the right place. I was thinking test to ensure it's called and throws errors when expected. Looks like you already added a test that ensures it's called, but still need tests for when errors are thrown.

> Is it a constraint for add2app that it should work without our generated xcconfig?

Oof, yes, add2app doesn't use a generated xcconfig. 

> Do a dry-run invocation inside KernelSnapshot to produce a native_assets.yaml there. 

So I'm a little confused. With add2app (Option A), there is no "outer" run. Apps are built and run through Xcode/Cocoapods. There is only the "inner" assemble command. Am I misunderstanding something?

Perhaps, in the "inner" assemble command, if `NATIVE_ASSETS` is not provided, it's does a dry run and non-dry-run if applicable? And then for non-add2app scenarios, we always set `NATIVE_ASSETS` in the xcconfig and if there are none, we set to the a value like `FALSE` or `NA` and skip if it's that value.

> Edit: A second issue seems to be that flutter clean removes the build folder

How does `flutter clean` come into play?

> > Edit: A second issue seems to be that flutter clean removes the build folder
> 
> How does `flutter clean` come into play?

That is the location the `native_assets.yaml` was in. So it's both deleted and the path is not being passed through xcconfig.

> > Is it a constraint for add2app that it should work without our generated xcconfig?
> 
> Oof, yes, add2app doesn't use a generated xcconfig.

Gotcha, yet another requirement gathered. 🙊 

> > Do a dry-run invocation inside KernelSnapshot to produce a native_assets.yaml there.
> 
> So I'm a little confused. With add2app (Option A), there is no "outer" run. Apps are built and run through Xcode/Cocoapods. There is only the "inner" assemble command. Am I misunderstanding something?

Correct, we only have an "inner" assemble command.

> Perhaps, in the "inner" assemble command, if `NATIVE_ASSETS` is not provided, it's does a dry run and non-dry-run if applicable? And then for non-add2app scenarios, we always set `NATIVE_ASSETS` in the xcconfig and if there are none, we set to the a value like `FALSE` or `NA` and skip if it's that value.

The approach I'm working on right now is

>> 2. Make the KernelSnapshot target depend on the NativeAssets target and use the non-dry-run native_assets.yaml. The downside of this is that now KernelSnapshot caching doesn't work anymore. NativeAssets targets don't have caching.

This seems to work as intended almost all tests pass. As you can see in the latest failures on the CI there's one thing not working. Build caching does no longer work because the `NativeAssets` target hasn't implemented that.

I believe the right solution is to write out a deps file with the files that were read during compilation of native assets, so that the Flutter build system can skip the `NativeAssets` target if none of the files were changed. We already have tracking of which files were read during builds, but we need to make this list of files available to flutter_tools (https://github.com/dart-lang/native/issues/105).

edit: The issue was not writing a `native_assets.yaml` if the experiment was not enabled 🙈

Add2app is now also fully supported and tested. (Using the `native_assets.yaml` that is created inside `flutter assemble`.) This also removed a bunch of code for `flutter build` because it now doesn't have to know about native assets at all. Only the backend `flutter assemble`+wrapper scripts and `flutter run` (for hot restart support) need to know about native assets. @vashworth could you review the PR again?
